narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 41, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you brought some impressive credentials to your law school application. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 75% probability that you would fail the bar on your first try. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3, which placed you in the lower third of your peers and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as it signals weaker performance in later undergraduate years compared to typical students who rank around the 5.5 decile. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 5, around the average of 5.71, but it still nudged the outlook slightly toward failure rather than overriding other concerns. Your standout LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward bar passage success and showing your potential in a way that many applicants lack. Overall, while your test scores and GPA compare favorably to averages, the model weighed your dip in later-year school rankings as the biggest red flag, outweighing those positives for this high-stakes forecast. This doesn't reflect your full potential or effort—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate options or targeted academic support, and reapply after demonstrating stronger consistent performance. Gathering letters from professors who can speak to your later-year growth could also help contextualize your profile for future reviews. With your LSAT strength as a foundation, focusing on building that academic trajectory will make a real difference next time.
